content: The lines $x+2 \mathrm{a} y+\mathrm{a}=0, x+3 \mathrm{~b} y+\mathrm{b}=0$, $x+4 c y+c=0$ are concurrent then $a, b, c$ are in
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since the lines are concurrent }\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore & \left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 a & a \\ 1 & 3 b & b \\ 1 & 4 c & c \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1(3 b c-4 b c)-2 a(c-b)+a(4 c-3 b)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-b c-2 a c+2 a b+4 a c-3 a b=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a c-a b-b c=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a c=a b+b c \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{b}=\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{c} \\ & \Rightarrow a, b, c \text { are in H.P. } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: $\mathrm{S}_1=\sum_\limits{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{r}, \mathrm{S}_2=\sum_\limits{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{r}^2$ and $\mathrm{S}_3=\sum_\limits{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{r}^3$, then the value of $\lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{s_1\left(1+\frac{s_3}{4}\right)}{s_2^2}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & S_1=\sum r=\frac{r(r+1)}{2} \\ & S_2=\sum r^2=\frac{r(r+1)(2 r+1)}{6} \\ & \text { and } S_3=\sum r^3=\left(\frac{r(r+1)}{2}\right)^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \lim _{r \rightarrow \infty} \frac{S_1\left(1+\frac{S_3}{4}\right)}{S_2^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{r \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{r(r+1)}{2}\left(1+\frac{r^2(r+1)^2}{16}\right)}{\frac{r^2(r+1)^2(2 r+1)^2}{36}} \\ & =\lim _{r \rightarrow \infty} \frac{r(r+1)}{2}\left(1+\frac{r^2(r+1)^2}{16}\right) \div \frac{r^2(r+1)^2(2 r+1)^2}{36} \\ & =\lim _{r \rightarrow \infty} \frac{r(r+1)}{2}\left(1+\frac{r^2(r+1)^2}{16}\right) \times \frac{36}{r^2(r+1)^2(2 r+1)^2} \\ & =\lim _{r \rightarrow \infty} 1\left(1+\frac{r^2(r+1)^2}{16}\right) \times \frac{18}{r(r+1)(2 r+1)^2} \\ & =18 \lim _{r \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{16+r^2(r+1)^2}{16}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{r(r+1)(2 r+1)^2} \\ & =\frac{18}{16} \lim _{r \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{16}{r^4}+\left(1+\frac{1}{r}\right)^2\right) \cdot \frac{1}{(1)\left(1+\frac{1}{r}\right)\left(2+\frac{1}{r}\right)^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{18}{16}\left(0+(1+0)^2\right)^2\left(\frac{1}{1(1+0)(2+0)^2}\right) \\ & =\frac{18}{16}(1) \times\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) \\ & =\frac{18}{64}=\frac{9}{32} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x, y, z$$ are in A.P. and $$\tan ^{-1} x, \tan ^{-1} y$$ and $$\tan ^{-1} z$$ are also in A.P., then
explanation:
Given, $$x, y, \mathrm{z}$$ are in A.P.

$$\therefore \quad 2 y=x+z$$ ..... (i)

Also,

$$\begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1} x, \tan ^{-1} y, \tan ^{-1} z \text { are in A.P. } \\ \therefore \quad & 2 \tan ^{-1} y=\tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1} z \\ \Rightarrow & \quad \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y}{1-y^2}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+z}{1-x z}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \quad \frac{2 y}{1-y^2}=\frac{x+z}{1-x z} \\ \Rightarrow & \quad \frac{2 y}{1-y^2}=\frac{2 y}{1-x z} \quad ... [\text{From (i)}]\\ \Rightarrow & \quad 1-y^2=1-x z \\ \Rightarrow & \quad y^2=x z \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad x, y, z$$ are in G.P. ..... (ii)

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$x=y=z$$

content: If for an arithmetic progression, 9 times nineth term is equal to 13 times thirteenth term, then value of twenty second term is
explanation:
To find the value of the twenty-second term in the given arithmetic progression (AP), we first use the given condition that 9 times the ninth term is equal to 13 times the thirteenth term. Recall that the $n^{th}$ term of an AP can be expressed as:

$$a_n = a + (n-1)d$$

where $a$ is the first term, $d$ is the common difference, and $n$ is the term number in the progression.

Thus, we can write the condition for the 9th and 13th terms as:

$$9[a + (9-1)d] = 13[a + (13-1)d]$$

Simplifying the equation:

$$9(a + 8d) = 13(a + 12d)$$

$$9a + 72d = 13a + 156d$$

Rearranging the terms to solve for $a$ and $d$:

$$13a - 9a = 72d - 156d$$

$$4a = -84d$$

$$a = -21d$$

While this relation between $a$ and $d$ is useful, it's not directly needed to find the $22^{nd}$ term, because it does not depend on the value of $a$ specifically, only on the property that links $a$ and $d$. Instead, we need to concern ourselves with finding $a_{22}$, knowing the general nature of the AP from the given condition allows us to progress.

To find the twenty-second term, we apply the formula for the $n^{th}$ term again:

$$a_{22} = a + (22-1)d$$

$$a_{22} = a + 21d$$

Given the relation we found between $a$ and $d$:

$$a_{22} = -21d + 21d$$

$$a_{22} = 0$$

Therefore, the twenty-second term of the given arithmetic progression is 0, which matches Option B.

$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \text { We have, } \quad S_n & =5\left(2^n-1\right) \\ \text { We know that, } a_n & =S_n-S_{n-1} \\ & =5\left(2^2-1\right)-5\left(2^{n-1}-1\right) \\ & =5\left(2^n-2^{n-1}\right) \\ & =5\left(2^{n-1}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $A, B, C$ are $p^{\text {th }}, q^{\text {th }}$ and $r^{\text {th }}$ terms of a GP respectively then $A^{q-r} \cdot B^{r-p} \cdot C^{p-q}=\ldots \ldots$
explanation:
Let first term and common ratio of a GP are $m$ and $n$ respectively.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Then,}\quad & A=m\left(n^{\rho-1}\right)\quad\text{.... (i)} \\ & B=m\left(n^{q-1}\right) \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ \text{and}\quad & C=m\left(n^{r-1}\right) \quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } A^{q-r} \cdot B^{r-p}, C^{p-q} \\ & =m^{(q-r)} \cdot n^{(q-r)(p-1)} \cdot m^{(r-p)} \cdot n^{(r-p)(q-1)} \cdot m^{(p-q)} . n^{(p-q)(r-1)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =m^{q-r+r-p+p-q} \cdot n^{q p-q-r p+r+r q-r-p q+p+p r-p-q r+q} \\ & =m^0 \cdot n^0=1 \times 1=1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text {We have, } \quad \sum_{r=1}^n(2 r+1)=440 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3+5+7 \ldots+(2 n+1)=440 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{n}{2}[2 \times 3+(n-1)(2)]=440 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad n(3+n-1)=440 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad n(n+2)=440 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad n=20 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the sum of an infinite GP be 9 and sum of first two terms be 5 then their common ratio is ..........
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Sum of infinite GP, } \frac{a}{1-r}=9 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a=9(1-r)\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

and sum of first two terms of a GP in 5

$$\begin{array}{lrl} \Rightarrow & a+a r & =5 \\ \Rightarrow & a(1+r) & =5 \\ \Rightarrow & 9(1-r)(1+r) & =5 \\ \Rightarrow & 1-r^2 & =\frac{5}{9} \Rightarrow r^2=\frac{4}{9} \Rightarrow r=\frac{2}{3} \end{array}$$

content: For a GP, if $(m+n)^{\text {th }}$ term is $p$ and $(m-n)^{\mathrm{th}}$ term is $q$, then $m^{\text {th }}$ term is ......
explanation: In any GP if $(m+n)^{\text {th }}$ term i.e. $a_{m+n}=p$ and $(m-n)^{\text {th }}$ term i.e. $a_{m-n}=q$, then $a_m=\sqrt{p q}$
